af_score_components.notes: MCP server exists but quality is limited (55). Early version (0.0.2) with Chinese-language interface and documentation. High-risk operations (modifying cloud configurations, honey token deployment) require careful handling. Credentials via env vars. Module coverage varies significantly by Chinese cloud provider. Unstable API surface expected at this version.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforcement depends on Chinese cloud providers (varies). API key via env vars — not persisted by tool (good). No scope granularity. Very early version (0.0.2) increases supply-chain risk. Pentest tool that modifies cloud configs — high blast radius if misused. No compliance certifications.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Open source tool with no SLA. v0.0.2 — extremely early stage with expected API instability. No documented migration path or breaking change policy. High-risk operations (config modification) with no idempotency guarantees. For security research/assessment only, not production automation.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. OpenAPI spec available. Two separate auth systems (Cloud Management API vs PostgreSQL connection) require careful setup. Transaction retries required for serializable isolation — agents must implement retry loops. Serverless RU billing can spike with unoptimized queries. PostgreSQL-compatible with subtle differences.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on all connections. Service account API keys with role-based permissions and scoping. PostgreSQL connections use standard pg auth (username/password or client certs). SOC2/ISO27001/HIPAA/GDPR/PCI-DSS — comprehensive compliance suite. Separate auth systems for management vs data access is good security design.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable API versioning with active development. Transaction retry is built into the design (not a gotcha). Retry guidance documented. Multi-region clusters provide strong fault tolerance. Serverless RU model requires query optimization awareness.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Very simple API key auth — highly agent-friendly. OpenAPI spec available but generated SDK is not officially maintained. Row IDs are not stable — can change on reorder. No native upsert requires query-then-create pattern. Rate limit (100 req/min) is restrictive for agents processing many rows. Formula columns are read-only via API.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Workspace-scoped API tokens with no further granularity — token grants access to entire workspace. SOC2/GDPR compliance. Token management via Coda settings. Simple key-in-header auth with limited security configuration options.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Backward compat generally maintained. No retry guidance, no idempotency for write operations — duplicate rows possible on retry. Webhook delivery not guaranteed. Rate limit complicates recovery strategies.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with moderate quality (68). Requires Docker for Memgraph — hard dependency that breaks in serverless environments. Shell command execution tool is a security risk if not sandboxed. Real-time updater recalculates all CALL relationships on any file change (performance issue on large repos). Some language parsers still in development. Can run fully locally with Ollama.

security_score_components.security_notes: Shell command execution tool poses significant security risk without sandboxing. LLM API keys in .env file (not ideal for production). Unknown version and unknown dependency audit status. No scope granularity. TLS only on external LLM API calls. Local Memgraph instance has no auth by default.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Open source with no SLA. Unknown version — API surface unstable. Docker + Memgraph dependencies create significant operational complexity. No documented migration path. Performance concern on large repos (recalculates all CALLS on file change). Suitable for development use, not production pipelines.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. OpenAPI spec available. Simple API key auth. Coverage data may lag CI runs — agents must poll for report readiness. API is read-only — upload happens via CI integration (separate concern). Repository identified by owner/repo slug. Acquired by Sentry — product direction may shift; verify documentation currency.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API token grants access to all repos the user has access to — no fine-grained scope. SOC2/GDPR compliance. Sentry acquisition does not materially change security posture. Token-based auth via Authorization header is standard and agent-friendly.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Read-only API has full GET idempotency. Coverage data lag after CI runs requires polling pattern. No retry guidance documented. Acquisition by Sentry introduces some uncertainty about long-term API stability.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server exists but poorly documented (quality 50). Multiple moving parts (SearxNG, LLM, embeddings, FastAPI) increase failure surface. YouTube/Reddit access depends on public API availability. LLM API keys in model_config.json. Local PDF support only. Windows requires WSL. Research/prototype tool, not production-grade.

security_score_components.security_notes: API keys stored in model_config.json (not environment variables — worse practice). No scope granularity. Unknown dependency audit status. Research project with no security hardening. TLS only on external LLM/search API calls. SearxNG local instance may be unencrypted.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Open source research project with no SLA. Unknown version — highly unstable. Multiple external dependencies (YouTube, Reddit, maps) can fail independently. No documented migration path. Multiple points of failure make production use risky. For prototyping only.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Excellent documentation with OpenAPI spec and multi-language SDK support. API format differs from OpenAI — not drop-in compatible. Tool use format is Cohere-specific. Embedding dimension changes require full re-indexing. Rerank endpoint rate-limited separately. Trial key has strict limits. Enterprise-grade RAG and reranking capabilities.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple Bearer token API key model — no scope granularity. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/HIPAA certified. COHERE_API_KEY environment variable pattern is standard. Enterprise deployment options available. Single key model limits least-privilege principle but is industry-standard for AI APIs.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioning with multi-language SDK consistency. Good migration guidance across API versions. Retry guidance documented. Full idempotency on inference calls. Connectors API for RAG has different latency characteristics to account for.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Free tier available without API key — very low barrier for read-only crypto data. CoinGecko-specific coin ID slugs must be resolved from ticker symbols before querying. Free tier rate limits are very restrictive for production agents. Pro plan required for DeFi/on-chain data. Offset pagination is standard.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key in custom header — no scope granularity. GDPR compliant. No OAuth flow. Many endpoints are publicly accessible without auth — appropriate for public market data. API key optional on free tier reduces credential management burden.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Status page available. Stable API versioning with versioned endpoints. Data update frequency varies by plan — agents must account for potential staleness. Full GET idempotency. Some endpoints unavailable on lower tiers. No retry guidance but reads are always idempotent.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. REST API plus Python SDK (primary). Comet and Comet Opik (LLM product) have different SDK initialization patterns. Experiment IDs are auto-generated — store key after creation. Metric logging is append-only — re-logging creates duplicates in charts. Large artifact uploads need artifact API, not log_asset. REST API token format differs from SDK init.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key via COMET_API_KEY environment variable — standard practice. No per-project scope granularity — key grants full user access. SOC2/GDPR compliant. Comet Opik product has separate auth considerations. SDK-based auth reduces raw credential exposure.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page and reasonable SLA. SDK maintained with versioned releases. Migration guides available. Partial idempotency — metric logging is append-only so idempotent re-runs create duplicate data points. No retry guidance documented. Separate Comet Opik product complicates SDK versioning.

af_score_components.notes: MCP framework (not a server itself) — quality assessed on the framework's MCP support (75). Progressive tool disclosure is the core value proposition. Semantic search mode collapses large tool catalogs into two meta-tools. Python-only. No auth built in — implementer's responsibility. Session state needs backend support for distributed deployments. v0.10.0 — early but functional.

security_score_components.security_notes: No built-in auth system — security entirely depends on implementation. TLS, auth, and credential handling are the implementer's responsibility. Early version (0.10.0) with limited security hardening. Tool filtering via progressive disclosure is not a hard security boundary — agents with cached tool lists may bypass. Open source Python package.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Open source framework with no SLA. v0.10.0 — early but past 0.1.0 milestone. Breaking API changes expected at this stage. Session state management varies by implementation choice. No formal deprecation policy. Reliability of systems built on Concierge depends entirely on implementation quality.
